name: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
english: The Setting in Motion of the Wheel

questions.q: The Buddha opens the First Turning by warning against two extremes. What are they?
questions.a: The extreme of pursuit of sensual pleasures (indulgence), and the extreme of self-mortification. Both are called ignoble, not beneficial. The middle way is then announced — avoiding both, producing vision, leading to peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, nibbāna.

questions.q: The middle way is then given a specific content. What is it?
questions.a: The Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration. The "middle" is not a compromise between extremes — it is this specific path, which avoids both extremes by being a different kind of thing.

questions.q: The Buddha names the First Noble Truth and gives a specific catalogue of dukkha. Can you recall at least four items from it?
questions.a: Birth is dukkha; aging is dukkha; illness is dukkha; death is dukkha; association with the disliked is dukkha; separation from the loved is dukkha; not getting what one wants is dukkha; in brief, the five clinging-aggregates are dukkha. The catalogue is specific and exhaustive — not a philosophical claim that "all life is suffering" but a naming of exactly where dukkha shows up.

questions.q: What is the Second Noble Truth, stated precisely?
questions.a: The origin of dukkha is craving (taṇhā) — specifically, craving that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting now here now there: craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, craving for non-existence.

questions.q: Each Noble Truth is spoken of in three phases — what are they?
questions.a: For each truth the Buddha says: "This is the truth. This truth is to be ___." (For dukkha: fully understood. For origin: abandoned. For cessation: realized. For path: developed.) And finally: "This truth has been ___." This is the tiparivaṭṭa, the three turnings, twelve-fold structure. It maps out what work each truth requires.

questions.q: After the discourse ended, who was the first person to attain stream-entry? What did they see?
questions.a: Kondañña. He saw: "Whatever is subject to arising is subject to cessation" (yaṃ kiñci samudayadhammaṃ, sabbaṃ taṃ nirodhadhammaṃ). The Buddha exclaimed "Aññāsi vata bho Koṇḍañño!" — "Kondañña has understood!" — and Kondañña received the name Aññā-Koṇḍañña. The specific insight that constitutes stream-entry is named here.
